At a distance from the sea the soil changes from grey to red; in some places it is gravelly but fertile; farther back into the country its tint is gradually deepened, till it becomes what is called 'mulatto soil,' consisting of black mould and red earth. This sort of land is generally strong, and affords abundant crops of wheat, tobacco, corn, &c. It is succeeded in its turn by a soil nearly black, and very rich. This succession in the different soils which occur in the state, is throughout pretty regular and uniform. They stretch in the same order, in lines nearly parallel with the coast, not only across the state, but all along N, as far even as to Hudson's river.—The staple productions of G. are cotton and rice. Its sea-island cotton is of the best quality, and worth twice as much as that produced in the interior. Small quantities of indigo, tobacco, and silk are grown, besides Indian corn, potatoes, melons, olives, oranges, figs, pomegranates, and several other fruits. The forests afford an abundant supply of fine timber, consisting chiefly of oak, hiccory, mulberry, pine, and cedar. In 1840 there were produced in this state 1,801,830 bushels of wheat, 12,979 bushels of barley, 1,610,030 of oats, 60,693 of rye, 1,211,366 of potatoes, 20,905,122 of Indian corn, 371,303 lbs. of wool, 19,799 lbs. of wax, 16,969 tons of hay, 10 tons of flax and hemp, 162,894 lbs. of tobacco, 12,384,732 lbs. of rice, 163,392,366 lbs. of cotton, 2,992 lbs. of silk cocoons, and 329,744 lbs. of sugar. The manufactures of G. have not hitherto been very considerable. Of the pop. in 1840 [691,392], 209,283 were returned as being engaged as agriculturists, and only 7,984 as in manufactures and trade, and 2,428 in commerce. The people in the lower part of the state have not been accustomed to prepare even their own clothing, or that worn by their Negroes; but in the upper parts of the country the inhabitants manufacture the chief part of their own clothing from cotton and flax. The value of homemade goods in 1840 was 1,467,630 dollars; and the whole amount of capital employed in manufactures 2,899,565 dollars. The chief articles of export from G. are rice, tobacco, indigo, sago, lumber of various kinds, naval stores, leather, deer-skins, snake-root, myrtle and bees'-wax, corn, live-stock, &c. The value of the exports in 1755 was £15,744; in 1760, £20,852; in 1772, £121,677; in 1791, 491,472 dollars; in 1801, 1,854,951 d.; in 1840, 6,862,959 d.; in 1847, 5,712,149 d.; in 1848, 3,670,415 d. In return for her exports G. receives West India goods, teas, wines, clothing, and dry goods of all kinds; from the N states, cheese, fish, potatoes, apples, cider, and shoes. The total value of imports in 1840 was 491,428 d.; in 1847, 207,180 d.; in 1848, 217,114 d. The imports and exports are chiefly to and from Savannah, which has a fine harbour, and is the principal emporium of the state. See Savannah. It is stated in recent American journals that a considerable improvement has taken place in the mercantile resources and prospects of this state. "But a very few years since this state imported almost everything required for the use of its people, who for internal communication and transportation of its produce to the sea-board relied entirely on such natural facilities as the country afforded. Shaking-off the usual inertness of a slaveholding population, they have seriously set themselves to work to improve their condition in every possible manner, and considering their comparatively moderate pecuniary resources, have done more than any of the other states for the advancement of their own prosperity, by encouraging undertakings that are now bringing an ample reward to all classes of the population. Lines of railroads have been constructed, which open a direct communication with the waters of the Mississippi,—an advantage which no other Atlantic state yet possesses. These roads extend 660 m., and have cost 12,000,000 dollars, twothirds of which sum has been raised from individual capital; and another road, 385 m. long, communicating with Pensacola on the gulf of Mexico, will cost a further sum of 4,000,000 d., of which the state is only asked to furnish on loan 1,500,000 d. in its own stocks. There are 36 cotton factories in the state, and their flourishing condition is rapidly bringing forward extensive investments in the same branch of industry. The iron-mines are exceedingly productive, both ore and coal being abundant within 1 to 2 m. of the principal railroads, the former yielding from 50 to 75 per cent. of pure iron." In the grand convention of Philadelphia, 1787, the inhabitants of G. were reckoned to amount to 90,000. In 1790, the pop. did not exceed 82,548, of whom 29,264 were slaves. In 1800 it was oured. The original pop. of G. consisted chiefly of Muskogee or Creek Indians, Seminoles, Chactaws, Chicasaws, and Cherokees. The Muskogees had their residence principally in its middle parts, and were reckoned the most numerous of the Indian tribes within the limits of the Indian states. The Seminoles inhabited the level flat country on the Apalachicola and Flint rivers. The other tribes were found chiefly in the W parts of the state. Of these, the Chactaws or Flat-heads occupied an extensive tract of hilly country, with large and fertile plains, between the Alabama and Mississippi rivers. The Chicasaws were settled on the headbranches of the Tombigbee and Yazoo rivers, on the N shore of the state, where they had an extensive plain country, well watered and of a pretty good soil. The Cherokees, in the NW part of the state, were the most advanced in civilization. All these tribes have been successively removed from the territory of this state.—The university of G. is located at Athens, and is designed to have an academic branch in each county; but a few only of these have yet been opened. It was founded in 1788, and is well endowed. In this institution and its branches there were, in 1840, 622 students. In the same year there were in the state 176 academies or grammar-schools, with 7,878 students; and 601 common or primary schools, with 15,561 scholars; but there were 30,717 free white persons over 20 years of age who could neither read nor write. The Baptists, Methodists, and Presbyterians, are the most numerous religious denominations. In 1835 the Baptists had 583 churches, 298 ministers, and 41,810 communicants; Methodists, 80 travelling preachers, and 25,005 white and 8,436 coloured communicants; Presbyterians, 75 churches, 45 ministers, and 4,882 communicants; Episcopalians, 4 ministers; Protestant Methodists, 20 congregations, and 15 ministers. Besides these there were a number of Roman Catholics, Lutherans, Scotch Presbyterians, Friends, and Jews. The first constitution of G. was formed in 1777, a second in 1785; and the present, in 1798. The governor is elected by the people, and holds office 2 years. One senator is elected for each county. The representatives are proportioned to the pop., including three-fifths of the people of colour; but each county is entitled to at least 1, and one co. to more than 4 members. The general assembly meets annually in November, at Milledgeville. All the free white male inhabitants who have resided within the co. in which they vote 6 months preceding the election, and have paid taxes in the state for the year previous, have the right of suffrage. The representatives in congress were in 1842, and still continue to be, 8 in number; the representative pop. of the state being in 1850, 749,043. The judges of Queen Charlotte sound. On its E side it sends GEO the superior court are elected for 3 years by the legislature; and the judges of the inferior courts, and justices-of-the-peace, are elected for 4 years by the people.—The public debt, according to the report made to the local legislature in 1849, is only 1,828,472 dollars; and, with a trifling exception, falls due from 1865 to 1874. The revenue exceeds the expenditure, is economically administered, and rapidly increasing. The governor recommends the adoption of a sinking fund in equal periodical instalments, giving it as his opinion that the entire debt might be discharged at or before the time it becomes due by an annual investment of 60,000 dols. Receipts into the treasury for the year ending Before the Revolution, G., like the rest of the S states, was divided into parishes; but since that period the division has been into counties, of which there are at present 93. The principal towns are: Milledgeville the cap. of the state, situated on the W bank of the Oconee 158 m. NW of Savannah; Augusta, formerly the seat of government, and Savannah, both on the river Savannah, the largest towns in the state; Sunbury, Athens, Hartsford, Macon, Forsyth, and Louisville. The situation of these towns is generally advantageous; most of them standing on the banks of considerable rivers, and some having good harbours.—This state has several important works of internal improvement. The Savannah and Ogechee canal, extending 16 m. from the Savannah to the Ogechee river, was completed in 1829, at an expense of 165,000 dols.—The Brunswick canal, extending from tide-water to the Alatamaha, 12 m. to Brunswick, was executed at a cost of 500,000 drs. —The Georgia railway extends from Augusta 165 m. to De Kalb co.; and the Athens branch from the Georgia railroad, 33 m. to Athens. The cost of the whole, including the Athens branch, was 3,300,000 drs.—The Western and Atlantic railroad, continuing the Georgia railroad from De Kalb co., 140 m. to Chattanooga, on Tennessee river, was executed at a cost of 2,130,000 drs.—The Central railroad from Savannah, 197 m. to Macon, now executing, is estimated to cost 2,300,000 drs.—The Monroe railroad extends from Macon, 101 m. to Whitehall.—The Ocmulgee and Flint river railroad, 70 m. in length, is designed to connect the navigable waters of these rivers, so as to form a communication from the Atlantic to the gulf of Mexico. History.] In 1732 the country between the Savannah and Alatamaha was granted by George II. to General Oglethorpe and others. He, with 40 others, landed at Yamacraw bluff, and founded Savannah, on the 1st of Feb. 1733. This state suffered much, in its earlier periods, from wars with the Spaniards in Florida. In 1752 the trustees surrendered the province to the king, and a general court was established in 1755. In 1763 George annexed the country between the Alatamaha and the St. Mary's to the province. Savannah was taken by the British in the revolutionary war, Dec. 29th, 1778. The town and state were evacuated by the British in July 1782. In convention, this state adopted the constitution of the United States, Jan. 2d, 1788, by a unanimous vote.
GEORGIA, one of the St. Lawrence islands, in Behring's straits, to the N of Oonalashka. It attains a moderate height, and is apparently volcanic.

GEORGIA (GULF OF), an arm of the sea lying between the E side of Vancouver island and the main, between the parallels of 48° 50' and 50° N, and the meridians of 122° 40' and 125° W. It is connected on the S with the strait of Juande-Fuca, and on the N with Johnstone strait and off into the mainland Jervis inlet, Howe sound, and [ Burrard inlets; and receives Frazer river. Measured from the parallel of 48° 50' along a line running nearly NW and SE, it is above 100 m. in length. Its greatest breadth is 30 m. opposite Jervis inlet, upon a line intersecting Texada island and Malaspina strait, which separates that long island on the E from the main. It contains several islands, amongst which Savary isle, Harwood isle, and Llasqueti, are next in extent to Texada. Both its N and S extremities present an archipelago of islands, through which various channels lead into the strait of Juande-Fuca on the S, and Queen Charlotte sound on the N.

GEORGIA (New or South), an island in the S Atlantic ocean, discovered by La Roche in 1675; and explored in 1775 by Cook, who named it Georgia in honour of his reigning majesty. It is situated between the parallels of 53° 57' and 54° 57' S lat. and the meridians of 35° 34' and 33' 13' W long., and extends SE by E and NW by W 31 leagues; its greatest breadth is about 10 leagues. It abounds with bays and harbours, particularly on the NE coast. These, however, from the great quantity of ice either within themselves or in the vicinity, must for the greater part of the year be inaccessible, or can afford but a very insecure station for ships. The whole coast is covered to the depth of many fathoms with frozen snow; and there was not observed a single river, or even so much as a stream of fresh water. The conclusion to which this not unreasonably led was, that there are in the country no perennial springs, while the interior parts, from their great elevation, never enjoy heat enough to admit of the snow being melted in such quantity as to produce a river or stream of water. So far as the interior parts of G. were observed, the appearance which they exhibited was found to be not less rugged and dismal than that of the coast. Not a vestige of a tree, or even the minutest shrub, was to be seen. The only vegetation discovered was a coarse strong-bladed grass, growing in tufts, wild burnet, and a species of lichen. Of animals, seals or sea-bears were observed to be pretty numerous. There were also flocks of penguins, of a large size. The oceanic birds were albatrosses, common gulls, that species of gull to which has been given the name of Port-Egmont hens, terns, shags, divers, and a small bird like the Cape of Good Hope yellow bird. The only land-birds were a few small larks. No quadrupeds were seen.

GEORGIAN BAY, an extensive sheet of water in Upper Canada, forming an expansion of Lake Huron, from which it is separated by a peninsula, terminating on the NE in Cabot's Head, and by Manitoulin island and a series of small islands. It receives on the N, French river, the outlet of Nipissing lake; on the SE, Moskoko and Severn rivers, the latter of which issues from Simcoe lake, and flows into Gloucester bay; and on the S, Nottawasaga river, which discharges itself into a bay of the same name. The shores on the N and E are flat, and the margins of the lake on these sides to a considerable breadth thickly studded with islets.
GEORGIAN ISLANDS, a name given by Captain Cook to the cluster of islands in the S sea, about 70 m. to the E of the Society islands, of which Tahiti is the principal.
GEORGIAN ISLANDS (NORTH). See PARRY'S ISLANDS.

GEORGIEVSK, a district and town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of the Caucasus. The district lies to the SE of that of Alexandrof and W of Mozdok. In the SW it is intersected by branches of the Caucasus chain, but towards the NE it is flat and sandy. The town is 90 m. SE of Stavrapol, and 216 GER m. NNW of Tiflis, on a lofty acclivity, near the l. bank of the Podkuma or Podkoumok. It is fortified, but is of little extent; and the houses, although built with considerable regularity, possess but little substantiality of structure. Pop., embracing that of the adjoining stanitza or military Cossack v., 3,000, chiefly Cossacks of the Volga. The governmenthouse, the churches, of which there are a Greek and an Armenian, the hospitals, the lazaretto, barracks, and public magazine of corn and salt, are the only public buildings worthy of note. The environs are pleasant, and the locality commands a magnificent view of the Caucasian mountains. This town was founded in 1771, and was formerly the cap. of the government.

GEORGINA, a county of New South Wales, bounded on the NW by Bathurst co.; on the NE by Westmoreland co.; on the SE by Argyle co.; and on the SW and W by King co., from which it is separated by Lachlan river. It is intersected in the N and towards the SW and S by two ranges of hills which run ESE and form the bed of Abercromby river, which has its source on the confines of Westmoreland co., and joins the Lachlan at the junction of Bathurst and King counties. Campbell river, an affluent of the Macquarie, forms to some extent its NE boundary.—Also a township of Upper Canada, in the Home district, bounded on the N by Lake Simcoe, and intersected in the E by Black river. It presents a generally hilly surface, but possesses much good land and excellent timber. Pop. in 1842, 586. -Also a small island in the Arctic sea, near Point Cheyne, in N lat. 66° 12′, W long. 84° 30′.

GEORGIOS HAGIOS, a large and beautiful village of Turkey in Europe, in Livadia, in the sanjak and 75 m. ESE of Tricala, near the NE coast of the gulf of Volo, and 11 m. SE of the town of that name. -Also a village in Rumelia, in the sanjak and 50 m. NE of Gallipoli, and 10 m. SSE of Ainajik, on the sea of Marmara. The manufacture of cotton fabrics and fishing form the chief branches of local industry. -Also a town of Greece in the Morea, capital of the nom. of Corinth, 15 m. SW of Corinth, at the foot of mount Polyphengos. To the E of this town are the ruins of the ancient Nemea.
GEORGIA (ISOLA DE SAN). See VENICE.
GEORGSDORF, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Posen, circle of Meseritz. Pop. 170.—Also a v. in the reg. of Breslau, circle of Glatz. Pop. 182.
GEORGSWALDE, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 42 m. NE of Leitmeritz. Pop. 4,500. It has manufactories of cotton fabrics, linendamask, and hosiery.
GEOURS-DE-MAREMNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Landes, cant. and 8 m. SE of Souston. Pop. 1,379. It has 4 annual cattle fairs.

GEPPERSDORF, a village of Prussia, in the reg. of Breslau, circle of Strehlen. Pop. 180.-Also a v. in the reg. of Liegnitz, circle of Lowenberg. Pop. 546.—Also a v. in the reg. of Oppeln, circle of Falkenberg. Pop. 378. GER, GHER, or GUER, a town of Marocco, in the prov. and 120 m. NW of Tafilet, near Ziz, on the S side of the Atlas chain.

GER, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Barenton, 9 m. ENE of Mortain. Pop. 2,612. It has extensive manufactories of pottery, a paper and an oil-mill.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Pontac. Pop. 1,865.

GERA, a principality and town of the state of Reüss-of-the-younger-line. The principality consists of separate parts, the largest and most northerly of which, containing the town of the same name, is bounded on the N by the Prussian prov. of Saxony, on the E and W by the duchy of Saxe-Hildburghausen, and on the S by the grand duchy of Saxe- Weimar. The 2d part, consisting of the forest of Pöllwitz, is chiefly enclosed in the kingdom of Saxony. The 3d, and most southerly portion, is in its greater extent in the seigniory of Lobenstein. They comprise a total area of 20,000 hectares, and contain, besides the capital of the same name, the towns of Langenberg and Saalboerg. Pop. in 1840, 22,700.— The town of G. is pleasantly situated in a valley on the r. bank of the Elster-which is here crossed by 2 wooden bridges-at an alt. of 228 ft. above sealevel. Pop. in 1846, 11,255. It is enclosed by walls, and has 2 suburbs. The streets are regular and handsome. It has 5 public squares, a palace, 2 churches, a fine town-house, an orphan's asylum, several hospitals, a college with a public library and museum, several schools, and a house-of-correction. G. has long been noted for its woollen and cotton manufactures, and printing-mills and dye-works. Linen, leather, hats, soap, tobacco, porcelain, oil, musical instruments, and carriages are also extensively manufactured here. The trade consists chiefly in wool, cloth, and drugs. In the vicinity are excellent bathing establishments.—Also a village of the duchy of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha, 20 m. SSE of Gotha, on the r. bank of a river of the same name. Pop. 670. It has manufactories of vitriol, potash, lamp-black, and paper.

GERA, or GEHRA, a river of Germany, which has its source in the duchy of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha, on the E side of the Thuringer-walde, and at the foot of the Schneekopf; runs through a portion of the principality of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, passing in its course Plauen and Arnstadt; soon after reenters Gotha, flows past Ichtershausen, and running thence N enters the Prussian prov. of Saxony; at Erfurt it divides into 2 branches, named the Wilde- Gera and Schmale-Gera, and after a total course of about 45 m., joins the Unstrut, on the r. bank.
GERABRONN, a bailiwick and town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Jaxt, 26 m. NNW of Ellwangen, at an alt. of 1,520 ft. Pop. in 1840, 751.

GERACE, a district and town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra. The district comprises 8 cant. The town is 36 m. ENE of Reggio, on a mountain about 4 m. from the shore of the Ionian sea. Pop. 4,000. The streets are narrow and dirty, and, although it contains some good houses, it presents a generally miserable aspect. It possesses a cathedral, 9 parish-churches, several convents, a seminary, and an hospital. Wine of a species called Greco, the produce of the environs, forms its only article of trade. Fairs are held twice a-year. This town was to a great extent destroyed by an earthquake in 1783. In the vicinity are several mineral springs. G. occupies the site of the ancient Locri, the ruins of which are still to be seen in the locality.
GERACI, a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Palermo, district and 33 m. SSE of Cefalu, in the Val- Dimona. Pop. 3,360.
